checks.command: work-packet.template.json and manifest.template.json are valid JSON
phase_template.yaml is valid YAML, validates against schema/phase.schema.json, no forbidden inline output field
every .md template starts with frontmatter containing 6 required keys
CONTEXT.md template includes '## Out of Scope' section
CONTEXT.md placeholders live in body only, not frontmatter
templates/ contains exactly the 8 contracted files, no stray extras

adversarial_check.description: Consumer-simulation check beyond the 9 listed commands, using python3+yaml+jsonschema: copied templates/phase_template.yaml and templates/manifest.template.json to the scratchpad and programmatically substituted every {{PLACEHOLDER}} token with a plausible, distinct real value (e.g. {{PHASE_ID}} -> '002-consumer-onboarding', {{STEP_ID}} -> '002.1', {{EVIDENCE_PATH}} -> '002-verify-01.txt') rather than trusting that the raw unfilled placeholder text happened to satisfy schema constraints. Both filled instances still validate cleanly against schema/phase.schema.json and schema/manifest.schema.json respectively -- this rules out a template that only 'passes' in its contract's own verification because the literal placeholder string coincidentally matched a pattern/enum, and confirms it also passes once genuinely filled in. Negative test: took the filled phase-doc instance (already proven valid) and added only an inline 'output:' field to its step, keeping everything else identical; schema/phase.schema.json rejected it, with the validator error specifically attributing rejection to 'additionalProperties' on the unexpected 'output' key -- confirming DD-3 evidence externalisation is enforced against a realistic filled document, not merely the placeholder skeleton.
